section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
paragraphs.text: Adevinta is a leading online classifieds group and champion for sustainable commerce across Europe and beyond. With digital marketplaces across many countries, our portfolio spans consumer goods, mobility, real estate, holiday rentals and jobs. Together, our marketplaces attract more than 120 million users every month and over a million businesses.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
paragraphs.text: Adevinta welcomes the opportunity to provide input to the European Commission's consultation on a potential Digital Fairness Act (DFA). We welcome the overall ambition of the European Commission to ensure a high level of protection to European consumers online. However, we believe that the current European consumer law framework remains fit for purpose to address many of the problematic practices identified by the Commission. Our response focuses on four areas that most affect our business and the online marketplace industry: (1) manipulative interfaces, (2) addictive designs (3) digital advertising, and (4) subscription cancellation and withdrawal. Across these topics, we support outcomes that enhance consumer protection, accessibility, privacy, and trust while preserving clarity and legal certainty for European businesses and SMEs.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
paragraphs.text: Beyond these four focus areas, Adevinta encourages the Commission to prioritise horizontal enablers that make the existing legal framework operate as a coherent whole: strengthen coordinated and consistent implementation and enforcement across Member States; and advance simplification— including the forthcoming “digital omnibus”—to streamline overlapping duties. Maintaining stability of settled concepts (such as the “reasonable consumer”) and avoiding undefined constructs will preserve legal certainty, while co-regulatory tools (codes of conduct, sandboxes, best-practice exchanges) and investment in consumer digital literacy and awareness can raise standards quickly without new hard-law layers.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
1. Manipulative interfaces (“Dark patterns”)
paragraphs.text: The existing legal framework including the DSA and the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) is exhaustive, fit for purpose and already addresses comprehensively manipulative designs (so-called “dark patterns”) both substantively and procedurally. The Digital Services Act (DSA) expressly prohibits deceptive or manipulative user interfaces that materially impair users' ability to make free and informed decisions (Article 25) and empowers the Commission to issue guidelines to clarify its application. In parallel, the UCPD captures misleading and aggressive practices, including in its blacklist (Annex I) (for instance, falsely claiming that an offer is time-limited). The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and ePrivacy rules govern consent's features quality and any storing or accessing of information on user devices, with sustained enforcement by data-protection authorities. Additionally, Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) sweeps (including in 2022) have also identified problematic patterns and triggered corrective action for companies that were in breach. In short, the existing legal framework is comprehensive and has been recently strengthened; we encourage the Commission and Member States to prioritise a consistent use of the tools already in hand of the enforcement authorities.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
1. Manipulative interfaces (“Dark patterns”)
paragraphs.text: It is our opinion that from an online marketplace -- but also from a digital -- perspective, principles are more effective than checklists to help manage newly identified consumer issues. Focusing on intent and actual or likely harm prevents inadvertently outlawing benign patterns that improve clarity or safety, such as prompts

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
1. Manipulative interfaces (“Dark patterns”)
paragraphs.text: that help users avoid fraud or flows that verify understanding of a contractual commitment.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
1. Manipulative interfaces (“Dark patterns”)
paragraphs.text: Practice illustrates that current rules already cover the patterns of concern: pre-ticked boxes for add-ons are addressed under UCPD rules on misleading practices; obstructive cookie-refusal designs breach ePrivacy/GDPR and have been sanctioned; advertising disclosures are mandated by the DSA, including labels, payer identity, and, for very large platforms, ad repositories.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
1. Manipulative interfaces (“Dark patterns”)
paragraphs.text: Additional prescriptive layers may reduce design flexibility, with a risk of potentially conflicting with accessibility standards (including WCAG and EN 301 549), and diverting resources from innovative European companies from improvements in safety, accessibility and consumer trust. Therefore, having new rules layered atop the existing rulebook may significantly add complexity and cost with limited incremental consumer benefit.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
2. So-called "addictive" designs
paragraphs.text: Concerns around excessive engagement warrant proportionate and evidence-based responses, as over-broad statutory definitions of "addiction" would introduce legal uncertainty and chill benign innovation.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
2. So-called "addictive" designs
paragraphs.text: Therefore, regulation should avoid creating incentives for privacy-harmful monitoring: mandates to quantify "addictiveness" or systematically track the behaviour of vulnerable groups could inadvertently pressure services to collect more behavioural and potentially sensitive data, in tension with GDPR principles of data minimisation and purpose limitation.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
2. So-called "addictive" designs
paragraphs.text: Adevinta believes that a better path is user empowerment: clear explanations and predictable defaults. For instance, in an online marketplace context, reminders for saved searches or price-drop alerts are often beneficial, helping users find safer, better-priced second-hand options. Where minors may be present, Article 28 DSA already sets a high bar for privacy, safety and security, complemented by the Commission's guidelines issued in July this year. The GDPR's principles of fairness, consent and minimisation further constrain engagement tactics relying on unlawfully obtained or processed data.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
2. So-called "addictive" designs
paragraphs.text: Lastly, we urge the Commission to consider that as e-commerce companies (and others in scope of the European Accessibility Act) simultaneously implement the European Accessibility Act, layering prescriptive UX obligations risks stretching resources and slowing accessibility progress—ultimately to the detriment of consumers that these rules aim to protect.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
3. Digital advertising
paragraphs.text: We welcome the ambition of the European Commission to tackle unfair and problematic personalisation practices, but the questionnaire seems to label online advertising as inherently problematic, overlooking its fundamental purpose. This contrasts directly with the Digital Fairness Fitness Check results, in which the Commission rightly acknowledges that personalization offers benefits to consumers (such as tailored offers and discounts). We urge the Commission to maintain this balanced view, distinguishing between manipulative personalization that exploits vulnerabilities and beneficial personalization that enhances the consumer experience and market efficiency.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
3. Digital advertising
paragraphs.text: In addition, digital advertising is already subject to extensive obligations that are still bedding in. GDPR legal bases, data-subject rights, and accountability apply fully to targeted advertising. The DSA adds robust ad-transparency duties, bans profiling-based ads using special-category data, and prohibits profiling-based

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
3. Digital advertising
paragraphs.text: ads directed at minors. Together, these measures set clear boundaries for acceptable practices and provide a consistent baseline across the single market.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
3. Digital advertising
paragraphs.text: We believe policy should promote privacy-enhancing technologies and at most focus on coherent guidance, rather than new prohibitions. This combination protects online consumers while keeping a viable path to compliance for smaller European players and marketplaces that sustain SME discoverability. Additional layers of rules risk reducing ad effectiveness, raising costs for SMEs, and driving revenue away from European publishers and intermediaries.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
4. Subscription cancellation and withdrawal
paragraphs.text: The principle that consumers can withdraw from and cancel contracts easily is already embedded in EU law and actively enforced.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
4. Subscription cancellation and withdrawal
paragraphs.text: The Consumer Rights Directive (CRD) grants a 14-day withdrawal right for distance contracts and requires clear, prominent pre-contract information, while the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) addresses so-called "subscription traps"—including, in Article 9(d), unlawful onerous or disproportionate non-contractual barriers when a consumer wishes to exercise contractual rights. In practice, these instruments give enforcers the levers they need to correct poor withdrawal designs and deter abusive practices.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
4. Subscription cancellation and withdrawal
paragraphs.text: At the same time, blanket refund entitlements or across-the-board cancellation prescriptions could increase the risk of fraud and undermine legitimate recurring models that enable low prices and predictable service for consumers. Therefore, any policy adjustment should remain coherent with consumer-payments rules and contract-law principles to avoid unintended conflicts and legal uncertainty.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
4. Subscription cancellation and withdrawal
paragraphs.text: Recent legislative updates (such as Directive (EU) 2023/2673, which will be in force from June 2026) have strengthened these guarantees, including the new requirement for a dedicated withdrawal button for distance contracts concluded via online interfaces.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
4. Subscription cancellation and withdrawal
paragraphs.text: Over-prescriptive UI mandates, by contrast, can backfire, as they may become outdated and reduce usability across diverse services, and risk clashing with accessibility standards (WCAG and EN 301 549, currently under revision), and fail to account for diverse service designs. Over-broad chargeback or refund schemes can also create fraud vectors, inviting opportunistic abuse and destabilising sustainable, low-price offerings.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
4. Subscription cancellation and withdrawal
paragraphs.text: Therefore, Adevinta believes that the proportionate course is guidance and consistent application of existing rules, leaving space for accessible, technology-neutral solutions, rather than imposing fixed layouts that could reduce usability without improving protection. Furthermore, before considering any new measures, it would be prudent to allow the Directive introducing the withdrawal button to be fully transposed and implemented across Member States. This will provide the necessary time for the Directive to take effect and demonstrate its impact on consumer protection.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
5. Horizontal measures and simplification
paragraphs.text: With an already very complete rulebook for consumer protection in the EU, the most impactful next step is to ensure the framework operates coherently. Therefore, we encourage the Commission to:

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
5. Horizontal measures and simplification
paragraphs.text: Prioritise consistent and effective enforcement of existing legislative frameworks across Member States. Structured cooperation improves legal certainty for businesses and outcomes for consumers and avoids contradictory interpretations and duplicative investigations.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
5. Horizontal measures and simplification
paragraphs.text: Advance the simplification agenda, including through the forthcoming “digital omnibus,” to streamline and align obligations across regimes, avoiding duplicate or conflicting requirements.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
5. Horizontal measures and simplification
paragraphs.text: Avoid redefining established concepts or introducing vague new ones. Stability of core legal concepts is a strength of the EU acquis. Broadening well-established CJEU jurisprudence concepts such as the “average (reasonable) consumer” or introducing undefined constructs like “fairness by design” would reduce predictability and complicate compliance without clear benefit. On the one hand, jurisprudence defined concepts strike a vital balance: shielding consumers from genuinely unfair practices while giving businesses the legal certainty needed to operate and innovate in the single market. On the other hand, embedding a new universal obligation to ensure fairness “at all stages” is conceptually vague and operationally very burdensome.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
5. Horizontal measures and simplification
paragraphs.text: Invest in digital literacy and awareness for consumers to strengthen informed choice.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
5. Horizontal measures and simplification
paragraphs.text: Structure regulator–industry dialogue and/or codes of conduct, regulatory sandboxes and best-practice exchanges to test practical solutions and iterate quickly where evidence shows risk.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: The EU already possesses robust tools to address manipulative design, engagement risks, opaque advertising and obstructive consumer contract cancellations. The fastest, fairest route to stronger consumer outcomes is consistent enforcement that makes the existing framework work as a coherent whole. New prescriptive rules would add compliance complexity and cost, restrict inclusive and beneficial design, and ultimately reduce the competitiveness of European marketplaces and the SMEs that rely on them. Adevinta stands ready to support the Commission’s simplifying, coordinating and guidance-led agenda to deliver tangible, near-term benefits for European consumers.

section_path: Adevinta's position paper on the Digital Fairness Act consultation
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Amsterdam, 23 October 2025
